subject: Physics
q:
A bird is flying close to the sea surface at a speed of 40 m/s. It decided to ascend and fly in a vertical circle. The speed of the bird varies with height h as

$v^2 = 1600 - 2gh$.

Its speed at the topmost point of the circle is 20 m/s. The acceleration of the bird in $\text{m/s}^2$ when its velocity is vertically up is [Take $g = 10~\text{m/s}^2$]
question:
A bird is flying close to the sea surface at a speed of 40 m/s. It decided to ascend and fly in a vertical circle. The speed of the bird varies with height h as

$v^2 = 1600 - 2gh$.

Its speed at the topmost point of the circle is 20 m/s. The acceleration of the bird in $\text{m/s}^2$ when its velocity is vertically up is [Take $g = 10~\text{m/s}^2$]
solution:
Let the bird fly in a vertical circle of radius $r$ centered at the point O . The height of the topmost point B is $h=2 r$ and the speed at this point is $v_B=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Substitute values in $v^2=1600-2 g h$ to get

$$ r=\frac{1600-v_B^2}{2(2 g)}=\frac{1600-(20)^2}{4(10)}=30 \mathrm{~m} $$

The velocity of the bird is vertically up at the point $\mathbf{C}$. The height of the point C is $h=r=30 \mathrm{~m}$ and speed at this point is given by

$$ v=\sqrt{1600-2 g h}=10 \sqrt{10} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

The centripetal acceleration of the bird at point $C$ is given by

$$ a_c=\frac{v^2}{r}=\frac{1000}{30}=\frac{100}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

At point C , the radial component of velocity is zero and tangential component is vertically up with magnitude $v$. The tangential component of velocity is given by $v=\mathrm{d} h / \mathrm{d} t$. Differentiate $v^2=1600-2 g h$ with time to get

$$ 2 v \mathrm{~d} v / \mathrm{d} t=-2 g \mathrm{~d} h / \mathrm{d} t . $$

Substitute $\mathrm{d} v / \mathrm{d} t=a_t$ (tangential acceleration) and $\mathrm{d} h / \mathrm{d} t=v$ to get the tangential acceleration at the point C as

$$ a_t=-g . $$

Thus, the resultant acceleration of the bird at C is

$$ a=\sqrt{a_c^2+a_t^2}=(10 / 3) \sqrt{109} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

subject: Physics
q: During the circular motion with constant speed :
question: During the circular motion with constant speed :
options: Both velocity and acceleration are constant.
Velocity is constant but the acceleration changes.
Acceleration is constant but the velocity changes.
solution:
During circular motion with constant speed, the speed remains the same but the direction of motion keeps changing at every point.

Since velocity depends on both magnitude and direction, the velocity changes continuously.

The acceleration in this motion is centripetal acceleration. Its magnitude is

$$ a = \frac{v^2}{r} $$

If $v$ and $r$ are constant, its magnitude stays constant. But its direction always points towards the centre, and that direction keeps changing as the particle moves around the circle.

So acceleration also changes.

The correct option is:

$$ \text{Option D: Velocity and acceleration both change.} $$

subject: Physics
q: Two racing cars of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are moving in circles of radii $r$ and $2r$ respectively and their angular speeds are equal. The ratio of the time taken by cars to complete one revolution is :
question: Two racing cars of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are moving in circles of radii $r$ and $2r$ respectively and their angular speeds are equal. The ratio of the time taken by cars to complete one revolution is :
solution:
If the angular speeds of both cars are equal, say $\omega$, then the time period of circular motion is

$$ T=\frac{2\pi}{\omega} $$

This depends only on angular speed, not on mass or radius.

Since both cars have the same angular speed,

$$ T_1=\frac{2\pi}{\omega}, \qquad T_2=\frac{2\pi}{\omega} $$

So,

$$ T_1:T_2=1:1 $$

The correct option is C, $1:1$.

subject: Physics
q: A particle P is moving in a circle of radius 'a' with a uniform speed v. C is the centre of the circle and AB is a diameter. When passing through B the angular velocity of P about A and C are in the ratio
question: A particle P is moving in a circle of radius 'a' with a uniform speed v. C is the centre of the circle and AB is a diameter. When passing through B the angular velocity of P about A and C are in the ratio
solution:
Angular velocity of particle $P$ about point $A$,

$$ \omega_A=\frac{v}{r_{A B}}=\frac{v}{2 r} $$

Angular velocity of particle $P$ about point $C$,

$$ \omega_C=\frac{v}{r_{B C}}=\frac{v}{r} $$

Ratio $\frac{\omega_A}{\omega_C}=\frac{v / 2 r}{v / r}=\frac{1}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is performing circular motion of radius 1 m. Its speed is $v = (2t^2)$ ms$^{-1}$. What will be the magnitude of its acceleration at time $t = 1$ s?
question: A particle is performing circular motion of radius 1 m. Its speed is $v = (2t^2)$ ms$^{-1}$. What will be the magnitude of its acceleration at time $t = 1$ s?
solution:
For circular motion, acceleration has two parts:

  • tangential acceleration: $a_t = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$

  • centripetal acceleration: $a_c = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$

Given,

$$ v = 2t^2 $$

At $t = 1\,\text{s}$,

$$ v = 2(1)^2 = 2\,\text{m s}^{-1} $$

Now tangential acceleration:

$$ a_t = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(2t^2) = 4t $$

So at $t=1$,

$$ a_t = 4\,\text{m s}^{-2} $$

Centripetal acceleration:

$$ a_c = \frac{v^2}{r} = \frac{(2)^2}{1} = 4\,\text{m s}^{-2} $$

These two accelerations are perpendicular to each other, so total acceleration is

$$ a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_c^2} $$

$$ a = \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{16+16} = \sqrt{32} = 4\sqrt{2}\,\text{m s}^{-2} $$

So the correct option is $\boxed{4\sqrt{2}\,\text{m s}^{-2}}$.

Option B

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in a circle of radius 1 m . The particle moves with a constant angular speed of $1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ about a fixed point lying on the circle's periphery. The velocity $v$ and the acceleration $a$ of the particle are
question: A particle moves in a circle of radius 1 m . The particle moves with a constant angular speed of $1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ about a fixed point lying on the circle's periphery. The velocity $v$ and the acceleration $a$ of the particle are
solution:
Let the centre of the circle be $O$, the fixed point on the circle be $Q$, and the particle’s position at any instant be $P$. The particle moves such that the line $QP$ rotates around $Q$ with a steady angular speed $\omega_Q = 1\, \mathrm{rad/s}$. This means that the angle $\theta$ made by $QP$ with a fixed line changes at a constant rate, i.e. $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = 1\, \mathrm{rad/s}$.

From the geometry of the figure, the angle between $OP$ and the fixed line is $2\theta$. So, as $\theta$ changes, $OP$ rotates twice as fast. The angular speed of $P$ around the centre $O$ is

$$\omega = \dfrac{d(2\theta)}{dt} = 2\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = 2\omega_Q = 2\, \mathrm{rad/s}.$$

This shows that the particle moves with uniform circular motion around point $O$ with an angular speed of $2\, \mathrm{rad/s}$.

The linear speed of the particle is given by

$$v = \omega r = 2 \times 1 = 2\, \mathrm{m/s}.$$

Its centripetal acceleration (directed towards the centre $O$) is

$$a_c = \omega^2 r = 2^2 \times 1 = 4\, \mathrm{m/s^2}.$$

Since the angular speed is constant, there is no tangential acceleration.

subject: Physics
q: The velocity and acceleration vectors of a particle undergoing circular motion are $\vec{v} = 2\hat{i} \ \text{m/s}$ and $\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} \ \text{m/s}^2$ respectively at an instant of time. The radius of the circle is
question: The velocity and acceleration vectors of a particle undergoing circular motion are $\vec{v} = 2\hat{i} \ \text{m/s}$ and $\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} \ \text{m/s}^2$ respectively at an instant of time. The radius of the circle is
solution:
For motion in a circle, acceleration has two components:

  • tangential acceleration along the direction of velocity

  • normal (centripetal) acceleration perpendicular to velocity

Given,

$$ \vec{v} = 2\hat{i}\ \text{m/s} $$

So the speed is

$$ v = 2\ \text{m/s} $$

Also,

$$ \vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}\ \text{m/s}^2 $$

Since velocity is along $\hat{i}$ direction, the acceleration component along $\hat{i}$ is tangential acceleration, and the component along $\hat{j}$ is normal acceleration.

So,

$$ a_n = 4\ \text{m/s}^2 $$

For circular motion,

$$ a_n = \frac{v^2}{r} $$

Putting the values,

$$ 4 = \frac{(2)^2}{r} $$

$$ 4 = \frac{4}{r} $$

$$ r = 1\ \text{m} $$

So the radius of the circle is

$$ \boxed{1\ \text{m}} $$

Option A.

subject: Physics
q: A disc of radius 1 m rotates about a fixed axis passing through its centre (axis is perpendicular to the plane of the disc). Its rotation angle varies as $\theta = \sqrt{2} t^2$, where $t$ is in second and $\theta$ is in radian. The magnitude of acceleration of a point on its periphery at time $t = 1$ s is
question: A disc of radius 1 m rotates about a fixed axis passing through its centre (axis is perpendicular to the plane of the disc). Its rotation angle varies as $\theta = \sqrt{2} t^2$, where $t$ is in second and $\theta$ is in radian. The magnitude of acceleration of a point on its periphery at time $t = 1$ s is
solution:
Let O be the centre of the disc of radius $r=1 \mathrm{~m}$ and P be the point on its periphery.

The angle made by the line OP with a fixed line OQ varies with time as $\theta=\sqrt{2} t^2$. Differentiate $\theta$ with time to get the angular velocity of the line OP (this is same as angular velocity of the disc because line OP is fixed on the disc) as

$$ \omega=\mathrm{d} \theta / \mathrm{d} t=2 \sqrt{2} t . $$

The rotation is about a 'fixed' axis passing through $O$. Thus, velocity of the point $O$ is zero. Hence, the velocity of the point P is given by

$$ v=\omega r=2 \sqrt{2} r t $$

Differentiate velocity with time to get the tangential acceleration of the point P as

$$ a_t=\mathrm{d} v / \mathrm{d} t=2 \sqrt{2} r $$

The radial acceleration (centripetal acceleration) of the point $P$ is given by

$$ a_c=v^2 / r=8 r t^2 $$

The magnitude of the acceleration of the point P is

$$ a=\sqrt{a_t^2+a_c^2}=\sqrt{8 r^2+64 r^2 t^4}=6 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 $$

where we substituted $r=1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$. Note that tangential acceleration for a fixed axis rotation can be written as $r \mathrm{~d}^2 \theta / \mathrm{d} t^2$.

subject: Physics
q: In a watch, the seconds hand is 1 cm long. The magnitude of the change in velocity of its tip in 15 seconds is:
question: In a watch, the seconds hand is 1 cm long. The magnitude of the change in velocity of its tip in 15 seconds is:
solution:
The second hand of a watch completes one full rotation in 60 seconds. Its angular speed is given by

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{60}\ \text{radians per second}$$

The length of the second hand is $ r = 1\,\text{cm} $. So, the speed of the tip is

$$v = \omega r = \frac{2\pi}{60} \times 1 = \frac{\pi}{30}\ \text{cm/s}$$

In 15 seconds, the hand covers one-fourth of a full circle (because $ \frac{15}{60} = \frac{1}{4} $). This means the direction of the velocity changes by $90^\circ$.

At the start, let the velocity be $ \overrightarrow{v}_1 = v \hat{i} $. After 15 seconds, the velocity will be $ \overrightarrow{v}_2 = -v \hat{j} $.

The change in velocity is

$$\overrightarrow{v}_2 - \overrightarrow{v}_1 = -v(\hat{i} + \hat{j})$$

The magnitude of this change is

$$|\overrightarrow{v}_2 - \overrightarrow{v}_1| = v\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2} \times \omega r$$

$$|\overrightarrow{v}_2 - \overrightarrow{v}_1| = \sqrt{2} \times \frac{2\pi}{60} \times 1 = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{30}\ \text{cm/s}$$

Hence, the magnitude of change in velocity of the tip in 15 seconds is $ \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{30}\ \text{cm/s} $.

subject: Physics
q: Two particles begin to move on the same circular path of radius 2 m from the same point P at t = 0. They have constant tangential accelerations $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ (clockwise) and $6 \text{ m/s}^2$ (anticlockwise), respectively. Let the point of their first meeting be Q. The smaller angle made by the chord PQ at the centre of the circle is:
question: Two particles begin to move on the same circular path of radius 2 m from the same point P at t = 0. They have constant tangential accelerations $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ (clockwise) and $6 \text{ m/s}^2$ (anticlockwise), respectively. Let the point of their first meeting be Q. The smaller angle made by the chord PQ at the centre of the circle is:
solution:
For motion on a circle, tangential acceleration and angular acceleration are related by

$$a_t = r\alpha$$

Since the radius is $r = 2\ \text{m}$,

for the first particle,

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{2}{2} = 1\ \text{rad/s}^2$$

and for the second particle,

$$\alpha_2 = \frac{6}{2} = 3\ \text{rad/s}^2$$

They start from the same point and from rest, so angular displacement after time $t$ is

$$\theta = \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$$

Take clockwise as positive for the first particle.

Then its angular displacement is

$$\theta_1 = \frac{1}{2}(1)t^2 = \frac{t^2}{2}$$

The second particle moves anticlockwise, so its angular displacement is

$$\theta_2 = -\frac{1}{2}(3)t^2 = -\frac{3t^2}{2}$$

They meet again when their angular positions differ by $2\pi$:

$$\theta_1 - \theta_2 = 2\pi$$

So,

$$\frac{t^2}{2} - \left(-\frac{3t^2}{2}\right) = 2\pi$$

$$2t^2 = 2\pi$$

$$t^2 = \pi$$

Now angular displacement of the first particle at that time is

$$\theta_1 = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

So the meeting point $Q$ is at an angular distance of

$$\frac{\pi}{2} = 90^\circ$$

from $P$ along one side.

The other side would be $360^\circ - 90^\circ = 270^\circ$.

So the smaller angle subtended by $PQ$ at the center is

$$90^\circ$$

Option D.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving along a circular path of radius 40 cm. At an instant of time, when the acceleration vector makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the radius vector, the speed of the particle is $2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and the magnitude of acceleration $\mathrm{a}=10 \mathrm{n} \,\,\mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-2}$. What is the value of n?
question: A particle is moving along a circular path of radius 40 cm. At an instant of time, when the acceleration vector makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the radius vector, the speed of the particle is $2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and the magnitude of acceleration $\mathrm{a}=10 \mathrm{n} \,\,\mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-2}$. What is the value of n?
solution:
In circular motion, a particle has two components of acceleration :

$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=$ centripetal acceleration acting towards the centre, along the radius and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=$ tangential acceleration acting along the tangent to the path.

The formula for centripetal acceleration is:

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}} $$

The speed of particle is given as $\mathrm{v}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and the radius of path is $\mathrm{R}=40 \mathrm{~cm}=0.4 \mathrm{~m}$

So, the magnitude of centripetal acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{2^2}{0.4}=\frac{4}{0.4}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

The net acceleration vector $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}})$ is the resultant of $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}$.

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{a} \cos 60^{\circ} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $10=a \times 0.5$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{a}=\frac{10}{0.5}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

$\Rightarrow 20=10 n$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2 $$

Therefore, the value of $\mathbf{n}$ is $\mathbf{2}$.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving on circular path and it's angular velocity $\vec{\omega}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and position of particle with respect to center is $\vec{r}=\mathrm{x} \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. Find the value of x.
question: A particle is moving on circular path and it's angular velocity $\vec{\omega}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and position of particle with respect to center is $\vec{r}=\mathrm{x} \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. Find the value of x.
solution:
For a particle moving in a circular path, the angular velocity vector ($\vec{\omega}$) and the position vector ($\vec{r}$) of the particle relative to the center of the circle are always perpendicular to each other.

When two vectors are perpendicular, their dot product is zero. So, we can use the condition:

$$\vec{\omega} \cdot \vec{r} = 0$$

You've given us:

$$\vec{\omega} = 3 \hat{i} + 4 \hat{j} + 3 \hat{k}$$

$$\vec{r} = x \hat{i} - 6 \hat{j} + 3 \hat{k}$$

Let's calculate the dot product of these two vectors:

$$\vec{\omega} \cdot \vec{r} = (3)(x) + (4)(-6) + (3)(3)$$

Now, let's simplify this expression:

$$3x - 24 + 9$$

$$3x - 15$$

Since the dot product must be zero for circular motion, we set the expression equal to 0:

$$3x - 15 = 0$$

Finally, we solve for x:

$$3x = 15$$

$$x = \frac{15}{3}$$

$$x = 5$$

So, the value of x is 5.

subject: Physics
q: A wheel is of diameter 1 m . If it makes 30 revolutions $/ \mathrm{sec}$., the linear speed of a point on its circumference will be equal to $N \pi$ (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ). Find the value of $N$.
question: A wheel is of diameter 1 m . If it makes 30 revolutions $/ \mathrm{sec}$., the linear speed of a point on its circumference will be equal to $N \pi$ (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ). Find the value of $N$.
solution:
The distance covered in one revolution is the circumference of the wheel.

Since diameter $= 1\ \text{m}$, circumference is

$$ \pi d = \pi \times 1 = \pi\ \text{m} $$

The wheel makes $30$ revolutions in $1$ second.

So, linear speed of a point on the circumference is

$$ 30 \times \pi = 30\pi\ \text{m/s} $$

It is given as $N\pi$.

So,

$$ N\pi = 30\pi $$

Hence,

$$ N = 30 $$

subject: Physics
q: An aeroplane revolves in a circle above the surface of the earth at a fixed height with speed $100 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. The magnitude of change in velocity after completing $1 / 2$ revolution is equal to 8 N (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$ ). Find the value of N .
question: An aeroplane revolves in a circle above the surface of the earth at a fixed height with speed $100 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. The magnitude of change in velocity after completing $1 / 2$ revolution is equal to 8 N (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$ ). Find the value of N .
solution:
When the aeroplane completes half a revolution in a circular path, its direction becomes exactly opposite to the initial direction, while the speed remains the same.

So,

  • initial velocity $= \vec{v}$

  • final velocity after half revolution $= -\vec{v}$

The change in velocity is

$$ \Delta \vec{v} = \vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i = (-\vec{v}) - (\vec{v}) = -2\vec{v} $$

Its magnitude is

$$ |\Delta \vec{v}| = 2v $$

Given speed,

$$ v = 100 \ \text{km/hr} $$

So,

$$ |\Delta \vec{v}| = 2 \times 100 = 200 \ \text{km/hr} $$

It is given that this change is equal to $8N$ km/hr.

So,

$$ 8N = 200 $$

$$ N = \frac{200}{8} = 25 $$

So, the value of $N$ is

$$ \boxed{25} $$

subject: Physics
q: Angular position $\theta$ of a particle moving on a curvilinear path varies according to $\theta=t^3-3 t^2+4 t-2$, where $\theta$ is in radians and time $t$ is in seconds. What is its average angular acceleration in the time interval $t=2 s$ to $t=4 s$ ?
question: Angular position $\theta$ of a particle moving on a curvilinear path varies according to $\theta=t^3-3 t^2+4 t-2$, where $\theta$ is in radians and time $t$ is in seconds. What is its average angular acceleration in the time interval $t=2 s$ to $t=4 s$ ?
solution:
Average angular acceleration is just like average linear acceleration. It tells us how much the angular velocity changes during a given time interval.

So we can write it as

$$\alpha_{av} = \frac{\Delta \omega}{\Delta t} = \frac{\omega_{final} - \omega_{initial}}{t_{final} - t_{initial}} \ldots (i)$$

To use this formula, we first need the angular velocity at $t = 2\,s$ and $t = 4\,s$. Since angular velocity is the rate of change of angular position, we get it by differentiating $\theta$ with respect to $t$.

$$\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

Differentiating the given expression $\theta = t^3 - 3t^2 + 4t - 2$, we get

$$\omega = 3t^2 - 6t + 4 \ldots (ii)$$

Now putting $t = 2\,s$ and $t = 4\,s$ in equation (ii):

$$\omega_2 = 3(2)^2 - 6(2) + 4 = 4\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_4 = 3(4)^2 - 6(4) + 4 = 28\ \text{rad/s}$$

Putting these values back into equation (i):

$$\alpha_{av} = \frac{28 - 4}{4 - 2} = 12\ \text{rad/s}^2$$

So the average angular acceleration during this interval is $12\ \text{rad/s}^2$.
